He received four yeses from the the judges and said: “I spent 10 years dyeing my hair, doing fake tan, because I was uncomfortable in the skin I was in, to change my mindset to stand in front of the world.
“So if I can do that, anything in your imagination can happen.”
KSI said: “You have superpowers bro.”
Holden added: “I would love an act like this to be on the Royal Variety Performance. So it’s a yes for me.”
Penman later told Ant and Dec: “To come from somebody that was ashamed to be who he was, tell my story and demonstrate my abilities, was incredible.”
Other auditions that made it through included Dom and his dog Ninja’s parkour act, and dance team LMA from Liverpool, who blew the judges away with their tutting dance performance.
Holden said: “We have so many dance groups that come on to the show that promise us difference, and they never are. And that was as different and exceptional as it gets. I’ve never seen anything like it.”
Cowell told the group they “nailed” their audition before bringing out the choreographer Sadeck, who he praised for “raising the bar” on the show.
Previous winners include magician Harry Moulding, who won series 18 and bagged the £250,000 cash prize and a spot on the bill of the Royal Variety Performance.
Britain’s Got Talent returns on ITV1 and ITVX next Saturday at 7pm

title: Chelsea: How many games will Wesley Fofana miss after Burnley red card?
content:
Chelsea defender Wesley Fofana is facing a suspension after being sent off in the 1-1 draw against Burnley.
Fofana was given his marching orders late on in the second half at Stamford Bridge for a reckless lunge on Burnley substitute James Ward-Prowse.
Referee Lewis Smith wasted no time in brandishing a second yellow card for the Frenchman, who was booked in the first half for a foul on Hannibal.
Fofana was perhaps slightly fortunate to have avoided a straight red card for his challenge on Ward-Prowse, but being dismissed for two bookable offences is crucial with regards to the length of his suspension.
Chelsea head coach Liam Rosenior, in response to the red card, immediately brought on Tosin Adarabioyo for Cole Palmer in an attempt to shore up the defence.
Later on Rosenior introduced three more defenders in Jorrel Hato, Josh Acheampong and Mamadou Sarr - for his Premier League debut - as the Blues attempted to hold on to the lead given to them by Joao Pedro in the fourth minute.
Those decisions would backfire on the Blues boss as Burnley took full advantage of the extra man, with Zian Flemming heading home Ward-Prowse's corner in stoppage time to earn a priceless point for the visitors.
Read More
As for Chelsea, it is now 19 points dropped from winning positions this season and six Premier League red cards as Fofana is left to reflect on his ill-discipline.
How many games will Wesley Fofana miss?
Due to Fofana being sent off for two bookable offences, he will serve a one-game suspension.
Had he been dismissed for violent conduct, then a minimum three-match ban would have been his punishment.
But in this case, Fofana will be unavailable for selection in Chelsea’s next fixture which is a London derby away at Arsenal on Sunday, March 1, 2026.
Fofana can return to action against Aston Villa at Villa Park three days later in what will be a huge fixture with Champions League qualification implications.

title: West Ham 0-0 Bournemouth: Hammers miss chance to boost survival hopes after goalless draw
content:
West Ham missed the chance to give their survival hopes a huge shot in the arm after a goalless draw with Bournemouth.
The 18th-placed Hammers could have seriously put the frighteners on the teams above them, as a victory would have put them level on points with Nottingham Forest and two behind Tottenham, both of whom have tough games on Sunday.
But despite dominating for large parts against below-par Bournemouth, they were unable to eke out a goal with captain Jarrod Bowen firing a glorious stoppage-time opportunity over.
Instead, the Cherries left east London having stretched their unbeaten run to seven matches, after the seventh successive draw in this fixture.
However, a point may still prove crucial to West Ham in the grand scheme of the relegation dogfight, especially after Bournemouth winger Rayan clipped the woodwork in the second half.
West Ham made a bright start, winning a corner after only 13 seconds from which Axel Disasi had a header cleared off the line.
Moments later a cross from Taty Castellanos found Crysencio Summerville, who steered his first-time effort straight at Cherries keeper Dorde Petrovic.
Read More
The effervescent Summerville was being watched by Netherlands assistant coach Ruud van Nistelrooy and looked in the mood to impress.
One of the winger's dangerous crosses had to be headed narrowly wide of his own goal by Adrien Truffert, before a second was gathered by Petrovic.
Shortly before half-time the 24-year-old, yet to be capped by his country, skipped around Marcos Senesi before curling a shot narrowly over.
The momentum was all with West Ham as Cherries boss Andoni Iraola cut a frustrated figure as he furiously scribbled notes from his seat in the stands, due to a touchline ban.
Disasi, rescued from Chelsea's bomb squad last month, has injected a quiet air of authority in the Hammers' previously angst-ridden defence.
With Evanilson and Junior Kroupi being kept in check, all Bournemouth had to show for their efforts in the first half were long-range efforts from Rayan and Alex Scott, both comfortably held by keeper Mads Hermansen.
After the break, West Ham midfielder Mateus Fernandes poked narrowly wide from another corner, before a first real glimpse of teenage Brazilian sensation Rayan almost put Bournemouth ahead.
The 19-year-old broke from his own half and cut inside before unleashing a vicious, curling shot which grazed the far post.
With Bournemouth now sensing they could get more than yet another draw from this fixture, El Hadji Malick Diouf had to make a superb block from Rayan and Hermansen held a Davis Brooks shot.
But they nearly found themselves when Bowen's cross was acrobatically put wide by January signing Castellanos.
Petrovic had to make a fine reaction save from former Cherries striker Callum Wilson and Summerville's drive was deflected over.

title: Searches continued at Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor’s former home on Saturday
content:
Searches continued at Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor’s previous home on Saturday, as the Government’s pledge to consider removing him from the line of succession following police investigations was backed by the Liberal Democrats.
Andrew was arrested on Thursday on suspicion of misconduct in public office after allegations were made against him following the release of files related to paedophile billionaire Jeffrey Epstein.
Despite being stripped of his title last year, the former Duke of York is still eighth in line to the throne and an Act of Parliament would be required to remove Andrew and prevent him from ever becoming king.
Sir Keir Starmer’s Government will consider introducing such legislation once police have finished their investigation into the King’s disgraced brother, the Press Association understands.
The Liberal Democrats would support the move to remove him from the monarchy’s line of succession, and party leader Sir Ed Davey told PA it would be “intolerable” for Andrew to succeed to the throne and that the scenario is “not as remote as some people think”.
Any changes to the line of succession would require the agreement of other countries which share the UK monarch, including Australia, Canada and Jamaica.
It is also understood that an influential group of MPs will meet to consider launching a parliamentary probe into the role of UK trade envoys.
Any inquiry would start by focusing on potential governance issues within the wider system and lessons to be learned, avoiding specific commentary on Andrew – who spent 10 years in the role – until there is no risk of prejudicing criminal proceedings, PA understands.
This weekend the royal family will be attempting to conduct their duties as usual, with the Prince and Princess of Wales due to appear in public.
On Saturday, Kate wore an England Rugby scarf to watch the team take on Ireland in the Guinness Men’s Six Nations at the Allianz Stadium Twickenham.
She was pictured chatting with injured England player Fin Baxter and RFU president Deborah Griffin in the seats before the game, in her first public appearance since Andrew’s arrest.
Read More
Meanwhile, Lord Peter Mandelson – who has also had his two properties searched by police – was seen leaving his north London home on Saturday.
A taxi arrived as the former business secretary was seen leaving his front door carrying a tote bag. He smiled at the taxi driver before entering the car, moving past a small group of waiting photographers.
Andrew spent 11 hours in police custody on Thursday, on his 66th birthday, before being released under investigation, after allegations he shared sensitive information with Epstein during his time as the UK’s trade envoy.
Detectives continued to search Andrew’s former home, Royal Lodge, in Windsor, Berkshire, on Friday.
He has denied any wrongdoing over his links to the convicted sex offender, but has not directly responded to the latest allegations.
After his younger brother’s arrest, the King said in a statement that “the law must take its course” and the police have “our full and wholehearted support and co-operation”.
Charles attended the first show of London Fashion Week on Thursday, hours after Andrew’s arrest, and did not respond when asked for his reaction to the news.
During a visit to a concert in Westminster that afternoon, the Queen also did not reply to a question about her brother-in-law’s arrest.
Thames Valley Police, who are leading the investigation, are yet to receive any “early investigative advice” from the Crown Prosecution Service in relation to the allegation, it is understood.
Detectives have asked Andrew’s former close protection officers to “consider carefully whether anything they saw or heard” during their service could be relevant to the investigation into Epstein and his associates.
The Metropolitan Police said they were working with counterparts in the US to establish whether London airports had been used to “facilitate human trafficking and sexual exploitation”.
On Friday, the force said no new reports of alleged sexual offences have been made since the release of millions of pages of documents related to Epstein by the US Department of Justice.
Scotland Yard previously said they were looking into allegations Andrew’s protection officers turned a “blind eye” to his visits to Epstein’s island, Little St James.
Police are yet to identify any wrongdoing by close protection officers.
Andrew’s primary accuser, Virginia Giuffre, alleged she had sex with him during an orgy with “underage” girls on the Caribbean island.
The former prince has previously vehemently denied all allegations made against him.
The 14 Commonwealth countries where the King is head of state are Australia, Antigua and Barbuda, the Bahamas, Belize, Canada, Grenada, Jamaica, New Zealand, Papua New Guinea, St Kitts and Nevis, St Lucia, St Vincent and the Grenadines, Solomon Islands and Tuvalu.
Andrew had stepped down from public duties in 2019 after his disastrous BBC Newsnight interview about his friendship with Epstein.
This meant he had already ceased to be an active counsellor of state, who can deputise for the monarch if he is overseas on an official trip or ill.
But as counsellors of state are drawn from the line of succession, removing him from it would officially resolve any question over Andrew taking such a role.

title: Dad's 5 words as he's arrested for murder after finding rapist in baby's bedroom
content:
Dad's 5 words as he's arrested for murder after finding rapist in baby's bedroom
Ben Batterham was at home drinking with a friend when he saw a man at the entrance to his seven-month-old daughter's bedroom at 3.20am. The intruder was carrying three knives, and had a previous conviction for rape
A father has spoken out about the moment he saw a convicted rapist coming out of his daughter's bedroom - and the shock twist that saw him convicted for the man's murder.
Ben Batterham was celebrating his 33rd birthday at home in Hamilton, Australia, with a friend on March 26, 2016, when an intruder broke into his property. Thankfully, his fiancée, Monique Cameron, and their seven-month-old daughter, weren't there that night, having gone next door to sleep in Ben's parent's house.
As the two men prepared to call it a night at around 3.20am, an intruder - a man named Ricky Slater - entered the property. He was high on drugs, armed with three knives and had a previous conviction of rape. Slater, who had only been released from prison three months earlier, headed for Ben's daughter's nursery where he snatched Monique's handbag.
As he emerged from the room, he was spotted by Ben - and tried to escape. Recalling coming face to face with the man, Ben, an apprentice chef at the time, recalled: "Very big bloke. Standing there. It was more than a shock. I was quite fearful for my daughter, and Monique, and at the time myself."
READ MORE: Chaos in courtroom as man found guilty of manslaughter – ‘smelly b*****d’READ MORE: 'Reasonable force' law explained as dad kills intruder in home invasion crowbar attackEnsure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.
Asked by 60 Minutes Australia whether his head was "exploding" at seeing the man leave his daughter's nursery, he admitted: "It was. It was doing backflips. I remember saying 'hey' and I've jumped up and started to chase him."
Ben pursued Slater along several streets and called the police before eventually tackling him to the ground in the hopes of making a citizen's arrest. A violent struggle ensued, and when police officers did arrive on the scene, Ben reportedly told them: "Give me two minutes with him." He then supposedly added: "I'll kill the dog."
According to Ben, the fight consisted of "a few headbuts, a few bites, he tried to snap my thumb back towards my wrist. Quite rough". He added: "I was yelling. I did strike him a few times. I didn't have him in a chokehold."
But, when cops arrived and handcuffed Slater, they realised he was unresponsive. Slater – who had scarring to his heart because of regular drug use, suffered liver disease, and was obese – had suffered a cardiac arrest after the chase, but was revived by paramedics. However, he had another two cardiac arrests in hospital and died the next day.
As a result, Ben was arrested and wrongfully charged with murder after begging cops: "Look, I didn't do it." Asked when the gravity of the murder charge sank in, he replied: "I would say when I was sitting on the truck, on the way to prison, like, this is really happening. I had lost my freedom, lost my job, lost my house, normality was just blown away that night."
Ben spent two months in prison after his arrest before being granted bail. His murder trial took place in November 2019, during which experts found Slater's death was caused by high levels of meth in his system and an existing heart condition after years of drug abuse. Ben, who has been diagnosed with PTSD, was found to have acted lawfully and was found not guilty.
The jury was not told during the trial about Slater's lengthy criminal history, including his imprisonment for at least four years in 2009 for raping a teenage girl after pushing his way into her home, and threatening to stab her if she did not keep quiet.
Justice Fagan said Ben should never have been charged with murder, and that a trial should never have taken place. He added: "Having seen and heard the evidence of all the eyewitnesses it does not appear to me that the restraint applied by Mr Batterham was excessive, putting aside the blows he dealt to Ricky Slater while holding him down.
"Those blows may have gone beyond the force that was reasonably necessary to restrain Slater and to prevent escape. But it has been clearly shown by every medical opinion offered in the case that they played no part in causing death."
Ben went on to file a civil claim against the State of NSW in the Supreme Court seeking damages for wrongful prosecution, and a settlement was reached. But despite being a free man, Ben has continued to suffer as a result of his ordeal, and his relationship with Monique has broken down. He said the whole situation was tragic, both for him after being labelled a criminal, and the man who lost his life.
Reflecting on the incident, he said: "You could say my biggest mistake was running out the door. But was that a mistake at the time? Someone breaks into your home, that's your castle, that's your home, people should always defend their home, no matter what." And as for regrets, he concluded: "No — I've done what any father would have done."

title: Sarah Ferguson 'seen for first time in WEEKS' after Andrew arrest as royal flees UK
content:
Sarah Ferguson 'seen for first time in WEEKS' after Andrew arrest as royal flees UK
The royal and her ex-husband Andrew have caused repeated embarrassment for the Royal Family over their association with late paedophile financier Jeffrey Epstein
Sarah Ferguson has reportedly surfaced in public after months of disappearing from view, and days following her ex-husband's shock arrest.
The former Duchess of York, 66, has been entirely absent from the public eye for weeks following the release of a new tranche of files from the Department of Justice capturing her correspondence with convicted paedophile and sex trafficker Jeffrey Epstein. Inclusion in the Epstein files does not indicate any wrongdoing, but she and former Prince Andrew have caused fresh embarrassment for the Royal Family - which has only intensified following Andrew's arrest on suspicion of misconduct in public office.
Ms Ferguson has now reportedly been seen for the first time surfacing outside the UK as she keeps herself separate from the furore surrounding Andrew, with whom she had lived until recently.
READ MORE: Major development in the search of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor's former homeREAD MORE: Sarah Ferguson 'feels the world is out to get her' as friends say she 'feels no remorse'It has now been reported she fled the UK as her former husband became further embroiled in the scandal, having not been seen in public since the Christening of her granddaughter Athena Mapelli Mozzi, the daughter of Princess Beatrice. She is said to have holed herself up in Zurich, Switzerland.
Ms Ferguson's absence comes as documents revealed correspondence with Epstein in multiple documents releases from the US Department of Justice, showing that she had spoken with the paedophile on friendly terms while he served time for heinous crimes.
Among the revelations involving the royal was that she had repeatedly emailed with Epstein while he was serving a sentence for child sex offences. Epstein was released from prison on July 22, 2009, having served 13 months of an 18-month sentence for soliciting an underage girl for sex.
In an email included in the files dated to June 14, 2009, just under a week before his release, a contact identified as "Sarah" emails him about one of the then Duchess of York's business initiatives.
The contact asks: "I need to ask you how I start The Mothers Army company so it can be commercial, how do I do that? Can you help me?" In a follow-up on June 26, she signs off an email with "love you", writing: "I am alive... yes I did go to the first lady and she loved the Mothers Army. I am going to call you later Love you".
Her association culminated in the closure of her charitable foundation, Sarah's Trust, which was "dedicated to supporting frontline, grassroots work to address the humanitarian and environmental crisis, the hunger crisis and issues perpetuating cycles of extreme poverty".
It was shuttered "for the foreseeable future", a spokesperson announced, just days after new details of her relationship with Epstein emerged. The spokesperson said the decision had followed "some months" of discussion.
They said it had also followed agreement between Ms Ferguson and her board of trustees, saying: "Our chair Sarah Ferguson and the board of trustees have agreed that with regret the charity will shortly close for the foreseeable future," a spokesman said on Monday evening. This has been under discussion and in train for some months."

title: Tourist bus crashes through ice into lake with eight dead including schoolchild
content:
Tourist bus crashes through ice into lake with eight dead including schoolchild
The shock incident on the frozen Siberian Lake Baikal was the third in just two days, Irkutsk regional governor Igor Kobzev said in a post on social media earlier today
Eight people have died in a horror mass-drowing incident after a tour bus crammed with tourists plunged through a floor of ice and into a freezing lake.
Russian officials said the busload of Chinese nationals fell through ice covering Lake Baikal, the world's oldest and deepest freshwater lake, as it crossed the body of water in southern Siberia. Officials said eight people died and one was able to escape the vehicle, with Irkutsk regional governor Igor Kobzev revealing that seven tourists, including a 14-year-old, and the bus driver were among the dead.
According to emergency services, while the incident took place at comparatively shallow part of the lake, rescuers had to use cameras to locate the tourists before launching a diving operation.
Russia's emergencies ministry said in an update that the bus plunged into a 10-foot-wide icy crevasse and through to a point in the lake that was around 60 feet deep. The water was so cold that the ministry's rescuers were forced to use underwater cameras to scope out the area before launching a diving operation.
Regional prosecutors have also launched a criminal probe following the shock deaths, with the Irkutsk tourism office reporting earlier today that the bus had allegedly been run by an unregistered operator.
The deadly incident will come as a massive blow for local tourism, which had been experiencing a boom under a mutual agreement between Moscow and Beijing allowing visa-free travel. Lake Baikal is one of the country's most popular attractions, seeing between three and four million visitors in 2019 alone.
The vast majority of tourists come from China, with data from 2025 showing people from the Russian allied nation make up around 51 percent of visitors.
The total for last year, however, dropped by around 1.6 percent on the previous year following the introduction of the visa-free regime. Sergei Romashkin, the vice president for Russia's Association of Tour Operators, said the new agreement would likely see a major surge in visitors during the summer.
The Baikal incident was one of several in recent years that has come with deadly consequences, and prompted a warning from Mr Kobzev on Telegram, who said the ice is "mortally dangerous".
He said: "I would like to remind you once again that going out onto Lake Baikal's ice is not just prohibited right now. It's mortally dangerous." Shortly following the incident, Mr Kobzev said in a post on Telegram that the tragedy took place after two others he grumbled hadn't "taught people a lesson".
During the previous incidents on Friday and Saturday, emergency services had to rescue six people who were trapped in their cars on the ice. Late last month, one Chinese tourist died when a car in which he was travelling overturned on Baikal's frozen surface.

title: Teacher's shame as she 'punches shopper in face over rotisserie chicken'
content:
Teacher's shame as she 'punches shopper in face over rotisserie chicken'
A school teacher has been arrested after allegedly punching a woman in the face in a supermarket while they were both queuing for a cooked chicken
It's hard to imagine being this desperate to nab the final chicken on the shelf.
A 42-year-old teacher has been arrested after allegedly assaulting a customer in a tussle for the last rotisserie bird in a supermarket. Police claim Ashley Murff punched another shopper in the face in front of multiple witnesses, and even spewed a vile racial slur.
The victim claims she was waiting in a queue for a chicken when she attacked by Murff, who was in line waiting behind her. The teacher's alleged racist comment is said to have sparked a verbal argument between the pair.
According to the shopper caught up in the row, while they were both arguing, she tried to snap a photo of Murf. It was at this stage where Murff allegedly punched the victim in the face, according to local media. The pair are said to have fought until they were eventually separated in the Valentine's Day incident.
READ MORE: Doctor and mum of two dies after becoming trapped in supermarket freezer overnightMurff allegedly left the shop - Sam's Club on Fashion Drive in South Carolina - before police arrived, but was identified from CCTV and a warrant was put out for her arrest, Law & Crime reports.
Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.
The victim's friend told a local outlet that her friend was waiting for a chicken and took an extra one for an older lady who was not able to wait in the queue. She said: "There was an elderly lady that didn't have the ability to stand in line, and my friend offered to just get a chicken for her. People are allowed to do that. You're allowed to get more than one chicken."
That's when the teacher allegedly called the victim a racial slur. "The situation highlights why it matters what people believe in, the biases that they carry. Those beliefs don't just exist in private. They show up in classrooms," the pal sa.d
The school Murff is employed by has reportedly announced that the teacher has been suspended from her position with pay pending an investigation.
Do you have a story to share? Email niamh.kirk@reachplc.com

title: Schoolgirl, 10, crushed to death after playground shelter COLLAPSES on five children
content:
Schoolgirl, 10, crushed to death after playground shelter COLLAPSES on five children
Lorrayne Rabelo Fernandes tragically died at the Ville Vitoria condominium in Vespasiano, in Minas Gerais state after a wooden structure collapsed on her and four other children
A 10-year-old girl was killed and her brother left hospitalised in a tragic playground accident when a wooden outdoor canopy suddenly collapsed onto her and four other children.
Lorrayne Rabelo Fernandes was found partially trapped beneath the beams of a pergola after it collapsed at the Ville Vitoria condominium in Vespasiano, in Minas Gerais state, Brazil on February 17. She suffered a cardiorespiratory arrest following the incident, in which heavy beams were sent tumbling onto a group of five children aged between four and 10;
The tragedy reportedly happened without warning as the group played, and, despite the quick response of emergency services, the young girl was sadly pronounced dead at the scene.
According to the Minas Gerais Military Fire Brigade (CBMMG), four other children were injured in the shock collapse, with one boy taken to hospital with a possible pelvic injury and suspected trauma to the spine. Another two young girls were treated at a local emergency care unit, and discharged with minor injuries.
Little Lorrayne's younger brother, Paulo Rabelo Fernandes, aged seven, sustained head wounds, and required stitches after the beams fell on him.
He remains under medical observation, but is reported to be out of danger. Local publications reported that the girl's father arrived at the scene to find his daughter already lifeless after the structure had fallen on her. The incident saw a massive local emergency response from several organisations.
Military firefighters, Service of Urgent Medical Aid (SAMU) emergency medical teams, including an advanced life support unit, and Military Police were deployed to the condominium following the collapse. The Civil Police of Minas Gerais (PCMG) said forensic experts examined the site and that an investigation has been opened to determine the causes and circumstances of the incident.
Police said only after expert reports are completed and further inquiries carried out will it be possible to establish the dynamics of the collapse and determine where any potential responsibility may lie.
In a statement, the Ville Vitoria condominium said the area was immediately sealed off after the accident and that it is cooperating with authorities. It also expressed solidarity with the families of the children who were impacted by the incident, but said it will not comment further until investigations are concluded.
Lorrayne’s body was taken to the Institute of Forensic Medicine before being released to her family. She was buried on February 18 at the Parque da Ressurreicao Cemetery in Vespasiano.
The shock incident in Brazil comes after a baby boy died during his own first birthday party earlier this month in the city of São Carlos, in Brazil's São Paulo province. Noah Gabriel da Silva Guimarães drowned in a pool at a farm in the city during the celebration, with his lifeless body found floating by his dad's sister-in-law.
While he was pulled out and given immediate medical attention, with a guest at the party administering CPR, he was sadly pronounced dead after being rushed to hospital.

title: Teenager's horror bungee jump death after instructor's chilling blunder
content:
Teenager's horror bungee jump death after instructor's chilling blunder
WARNING: DISTRESSING CONTENT Teenager Vera Mol, 17, plunged to her death in Spain in an avoidable tragedy
A teenage girl tragically died in a devastating bungee jump accident after an instructor's "poor English" led to a fatal misunderstanding.
Vera Mol, 17, from the Netherlands, lost her life in August 2015 after jumping from a bridge in northern Spain before her safety harness had been attached, following a catastrophic miscommunication.
The Dutch youngster was among a party of 13 Dutch and Belgian teenagers, all aged under 18, taking part in bungee jumping activities near Cabezón de la Sal, Cantabria.
Vera Mol was second from last to take the plunge and it was her maiden attempt at the extreme sport. She stepped off the bridge and fell fatally around 32 metres onto the riverbed beneath.
Before her leap, the instructor had called out "no jump" - words that the court heard she may have tragically mistaken for "now jump".
Her death could have been prevented had the instructor employed the proper instruction "don't jump", rather than the ambiguous wording.
The court determined that confusing language was central to the fatal incident and described the instructor's English as "macarronico", meaning "very bad".
It was found that the instructor's command of English was insufficiently competent for him to be supervising foreigners in "something as delicate as jumping into the void from an elevated point".
The instructor stood trial charged with causing Vera's death. Meanwhile, the director of the bungee jumping firm also faced charges of negligent homicide, according to contemporary reports.
Flowtrack, the firm operating the bungee jumping operation where the worker was employed, described the incident as an accident. Nevertheless, Martijn Klom from the organisation acknowledged her death resulted from a miscommunication.
He verified that she was given confusing directions and that whilst she was connected to the rope during her jump - she had not been properly fastened to the bridge.
Beyond the fatal language error, the court was presented with a litany of alarming safety breaches that determined Vera's tragic end.
The instructor had failed to secure the 17-year-old to any safety line whilst climbing to the bridge, meaning she stood unprotected just moments before her fatal plunge.
Judges also learnt the adventure firm had not established a secure waiting zone for the group, compelling the youngsters to remain at what was characterised as "the edge of the abyss" whilst awaiting their turn.
Staff members faced further criticism for neglecting to verify Vera's identification to establish she had reached 18.
At just 17, she fell below the legal age requirement to participate, and the business had not obtained parental consent before permitting her access to the bridge.
Vera's heartbroken relatives have subsequently demanded stricter safety regulations to stop another young person perishing in such an unnecessary and preventable tragedy.

title: 'My parents have been locked up for 10 years in Iran hellhole prison - but I've realised one major thing'
content:
EXCLUSIVE: 'My parents have been locked up for 10 years in Iran hellhole prison - but I've realised one major thing'
Lindsay and Craig Foreman have been sentenced to prison in Iran on bogus spying charges, but their family tell the Mirror they will never give up fighting for the couple's release
British couple Lindsay and Craig Foreman have been sentenced to 10 years in a notorious Iranian prison for espionage, but despite the heartbreak their children feel, there is no way they are giving up until they get their parents home. Lindsay and Craig, both 53, were embarking on a motorcycle trip across the world when they were detained in Iran at the beginning of last year, accused of being spies
It was supposed to be their dream trip, which would see them create memories to last a lifetime, but what began was a nightmare seemingly with no end. Lindsay - a psychologist - has two sons, Toby and Joe, and carpenter Craig has a daughter, Chelsea, and a son, Kieran. The four siblings at first had no contact with their parents for months, left unsure if the couple were even still alive.
After more than 12 months of being held in terrifying and brutal conditions, which have seen Lindsay and Craig denied adequate health care and endure violence and squalor in Tehran's notorious Evin Prison, the couple and their family are now facing a grim reality - the couple are facing 10 years behind bars.
Joe Bennett, Lindsay's son, tells the Mirror that despite his family are grieving, the sentence has only made them more committed to fighting for the couple, and that they are more resolved than ever to keep campaigning for their parents. "Ten years is a number that hits you in the chest. There's shock. There's anger. There's grief," he explains. "But there is also resolve. We've been public since July and we've weathered waves of uncertainty already. This doesn't break us, but it does harden our focus."
Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.
He adds that the family have had only "limited communication" with Lindsay and Craig since they were sentenced, and whilst those conversations were difficult, the main thing the unbelievably brave siblings took from speaking to their parents was determination to keep going. "We have had limited communication. We've managed to speak briefly since their sentencing and with that brought a whole array of emotions, but the one most prominent was resolve. That this is the next phase of the fight for justice."
Former hostages and their families tell the Mirror that this moment will certainly not be an easy one. "The moment of sentencing is surreal and crushing, when a few words suddenly redefine your future and devastate your family," explains Anoosheh Ashoori, who was sentenced to 12 years imprisonment on false charges back in 2019. The British-Iranian businessman was eventually released after more than four and a half years in 2022, alongside Nazanin Zaghari-Ratcliffe, after enduring horrific conditions in Evin Prison. Anoosheh had been visiting his elderly mother in Iran in 2017 when he was detained on bogus charges, blindfolded and taken away - since his release has campaigned for hostages and created moving artwork about his experiences.
Richard Ratcliffe, the husband of Nazanin who campaigned tirelessly to ensure her release, tells the Mirror that the moment his wife was sentenced was one of the worst days he has ever endured, and that Lindsay and Craig's "sentencing brings back lots of memories. For our family it was one of the hardest days.
"There was a real numbness, as all those years stretching ahead suddenly felt very real. They will be thinking about all the birthdays and weddings they are going to miss, and in time the funerals. Today it will feel like a life sentence for the whole family, and so unfair. The imprisonment will cast a long shadow over all their lives."
The campaigner advises that "for the next few days," the couple and their children can only "take it one day at a time. An experience like this is very isolating. So it is worth remembering they are not alone, and be reminded that lots of people care, however much it feels like the opposite behind those grey walls."
Joe gives some insight into how he and his siblings are managing to get through this harrowing moment, "We cope by staying united, by staying disciplined. By remembering that they are still here, still strong, and still deserving of relentless advocacy."
Both Richard and Anoosheh don't believe that the UK government has done enough to secure Lindsay and Craig's release, and think that the politicians have not learned from their past mistakes when it comes to handling hostage situations. "It's triggering for us," admits Richard, adding, "it is really depressing that the government has chosen not to learn the lessons from Nazanin's case."
Anoosheh says, "the British Government must end its passiveness and inaction, and deal with hostage diplomacy decisively and firmly once and for all."
Foreign Minister Yvette Cooper has called the 10-year sentence handed to Lindsey and Craig "totally unjustifiable" and said "We will pursue this case relentlessly with the Iranian government until we see Craig and Lindsay Foreman safely returned to the UK and reunited with their family".
Joe says that he and his family "welcome any intervention at senior level," but adds, "At the same time, this case has now resulted in a ten-year sentence. So naturally we ask whether earlier, stronger action might have changed the trajectory.
"Our position has always been constructive. We work with the government, not against it. But families are allowed to expect urgency when lives are at stake."
The conditions in Evin Prison are brutal, Joe says that they are desperately worried about the state of their parents physical and mental health. "Their health is a constant concern. Prison conditions are difficult at the best of times, and this is not the best of times."
Joe adds, "Craig had been in significant pain with his tooth for months. He has now received very basic treatment, but it was delayed far longer than it should ever have been. More broadly, they are doing their best to stay strong mentally and physically, but uncertainty takes its toll."
He previously told the Mirror about the dire conditions in Evin Prison - which has seen increased violence break out inside it since unrest in Iran earlier this year. Executions are commonplace, Joe explained, as the prison has become even more overfilled with the arrest of dissenters and protesters. Lindsay and Craig are being subjected to unhygienic conditions, with vermin and rats part of the daily landscape. He also revealed that fights regularly break out in the prison, where Lindsay is currently housed in the women's wing and Craig with the political prisoners.
As it stands, the larger geopolitical context surrounding Iran is "impossible to ignore" for Joe and his siblings. President Trump has sent what he dubbed an "armada" of military force to the region, poised to attack the Islamic Republic, as negotiations over the nuclear deal have been stalling. Months of civil unrest also have changed the landscape inside the country itself, with protests breaking out across Iran against the regime early this year, that were violently put down by the government. Iranian authorities said at the end of January more than 3000 protesters have been killed, but other groups estimate this figure to be much higher, saying the number is over 36,500.
Richard tells the Mirror that hostages are routinely used as weapons of "political theatre" and the sudden sentencing of Lindsay and Craig indicates that Iran wants the UK "to engage". He adds that in his own experience with wife Nazanin, "We always tried to remind ourselves that hostage cases are not real cases...But the pain is real, and so is the sentence now."
The campaigner dubbed the sentencing of the Foremans as looking "from the outside" to be a "human shield strategy by Iran in response to the military build up, and a warning by the UK not to sacrifice its own citizens." But for Lindsay and Craig's children, they desperately want the public to remember that at the heart of this case are two normal people, enduring torturous conditions, who should not be pawns in a diplomatic game. Joe explains, "What I will say is this: my mum and Craig are ordinary people. They are not politicians. They are not negotiators. They should never be caught in the middle of geopolitical tension."
International Relations expert Professor Anthony Glees from the University of Buckingham tells the Mirror that the UK government needs to act "immediately" to bring the couple home, because this is "a moment of extraordinary danger for the Foremans". He notes that the massive amount of military power Trump has moved to the region could "sustain a prolonged attack" against Iran: "Once the bombing starts, if the bombing starts, the UK loses any traction and the story of the Foreman's ordeal spirals out of control. That cannot be acceptable to any government that claims to believe in human rights."
The expert also calls the charges of espionage levied at the couple as "absurd nonsense". He explains, "Spies do not travel round the world on motorbikes using their own names and with visas and other formal documents in their pockets."
Support the campaign to Free Lindsay and Craig by signing the petition, and donating to the GoFundMe

title: Surfer bitten in half by 'car-sized' great white shark in front of horrified friends
content:
Surfer bitten in half by 'car-sized' great white shark in front of horrified friends
WARNING: GRAPHIC CONTENT Australian surfer Brad Smith, 29, died in a horrific great white shark attack near Gracetown, Western Australia, as friends watched helplessly
While great white sharks are responsible for relatively few attacks, their immense size and velocity mean that when they do attack, the consequences are frequently devastating.
For 29-year-old Australian Brad Smith, who was set upon by a great white while surfing, this harsh reality became heartbreakingly evident when the predator killed him before his friends' eyes in a brutal and haunting assault.
Smith had been surfing off Gracetown, about 150 miles south of Perth, when an enormous shark described by onlookers as "as wide as a car" struck him violently, snapping his surfboard clean in half.
The victim, who had travelled to the beach with companions, was reportedly dragged beneath the surface and began fighting desperately to fend off the creature.
Smith's ordeal intensified when bystanders spotted a second shark closing in on him, leaving the unfortunate surfer hopelessly outnumbered and overpowered.
Nevertheless, Smith was described as courageously battling the animals, launching frantic strikes in their direction. Heartbreakingly, his efforts proved futile, with the great white dragging him underwater within minutes.
Shortly afterwards, amid a crimson stain spreading across the water, Smith's lifeless form rose to the surface.
His companions made every effort to save him, even endangering themselves by paddling out to retrieve him and haul him back to the beach, but upon reaching him, it was already too late.
An anaesthetist who was surfing nearby confirmed Smith's wounds were fatal and that he would have died "quick and painless".
Authorities successfully retrieved Smith's surfboard, which had been severed completely by the creature's lethal teeth.
An extensive operation involving marine vessels and aircraft was deployed, however the shark responsible was never found.
Based on eyewitness testimonies and the rapid, violent nature of the incident, officials concluded the predator was most likely a great white.
Rod Draper, a close friend of Smith's, described him as an extremely well-loved member of the local surfing community. "He absolutely loved life," he said.
"There isn't a person who could say a bad word about him."
Whilst some surfers demanded sharks be culled, Smith's relatives reportedly urged authorities not to destroy the animal, maintaining they held no ill will towards it for the tragedy.

title: Inside Ukraine village where locals were ‘prisoners’ in homes under Kremlin rule
content:
Dead sunflowers line the road into a snowy Ukrainian village only miles from fighting.
But despite the hardship of overcoming brutal Russian occupation, life here has endured… so it might bloom again. Faint booms and gunfire in the distance offer a soundtrack to our visit to Kramarivka in Kharkiv oblast, north-east Ukraine. Military training takes place nearby, we are told; but to the untuned ear it’s hard to tell the difference.
The frontline is about 20 miles away, but this village was once itself a scene of terror. Grandmother Nina Zhdaniyuk, 71, who has lived here her whole life, recalls the pain of Kremlin rule.
People were prisoners in their homes; men stripped naked. “Russians entered our village on heavy vehicles with faces hidden,” she says. “People were scared and hiding. Russians have been moving in horrible convoys and I thought they were going to ruin our village.”
She adds: “They [the Russians] are f***ing animals. They did not allow us to leave the village. We were… locked in our houses. They stripped the men naked, even people with disabilities, and searched their bodies for traces of gunshot.”
This is an apparent reference to bruising left by the butt of an automatic rifle hitting the shoulder when firing – a sign men might be soldiers. “This was how they tried to find Ukrainian soldiers who, in their opinion, could have changed into civilian clothes,” she adds.
With Tuesday marking four years since Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine, Kramarivka was among settlements in the region taken before being liberated. The village’s occupation lasted from March to autumn 2022. “Actually, we were prisoners,” Nina says. “We had neither money, nor food. Nothing. Some people even baked a bread made of remnants of wheat.”
But she never doubted liberation would arrive. “We believed, we sincerely believed that we would be free and that this day would come. We believed all the time,” she says. Nina might be a warm character yet when asked whether she has a message for Russian leader Vladimir Putin, this brave elderly lady does not mince her words. “I wish him a torturous death. Him and his commanders. We didn't touch them — they came to us and kill our children. They are not humans,” she says.
During our visit, Nina walks us down to the humble home she shares with her husband and daughter, giving a tour of the village along the way. We stop at a local shop stocked with popular Ukrainian foods — pickled cabbage and dumplings. The village’s population before the occupation was 180, including 32 kids. Though now there are about 160 with 28 children, the youngest being aged six.
Married Nina is among villagers helped by British charity UK-Med, whose staff visit to provide health care. Nurse Katerina Lutsenko, part of the team visiting which also includes a doctor and community health worker, says: “We work in very remote locations where people are cut off from basic services, medical care. They cannot reach the nearest district hospital to get help. This includes de-occupied villages.”
She clearly takes pride in her work. “Unfortunately, the war continues and many people still need our help and medical care. We are doing really important work,” she adds. Another helped by the charity is widow Nadiia Sytnyk, who lives with her daughter and son-in-law. The 73-year-old has also called the village home her whole life – and she never wants to leave. Nadiia touches my glasses as she explains she used to work in a factory producing lenses.
READ MORE: Inside Ukrainian frontline village where displaced family have lost everythingShe offers a snapshot of the pain of occupation. “We were hiding, scared. It was extremely difficult. Sometimes we didn't have bread or anything to eat. What should we do? We ate what we found in the cellar,” she says. And the community rallied round each other. “If somebody had any food, they were sharing it,” she recalls. “Especially for families with kids.”
The day of liberation is not one she will forget. “We saw our militaries coming, we ran out of the houses. We felt like jumping onto the tanks ourselves. My daughter was crying,” she says. Nadiia adds: “We just wonder when this war is over. Nobody knows. Waiting for an end and looking forward to it. It's more and more difficult to move on and not to lose a heart. Difficult life but it is how it is.”
Nina vividly remembers the village’s liberation too. “Russians were escaping. We heard a rumbling and thought something terrible was about to happen,” she explains. "But it turned out to be a tank under the Ukrainian flag. From that moment on, we were able to leave the village, and they began to bring us bread and humanitarian aid.”
But Nina does not just want to survive. “I want our village to flourish,” she says. “I want peace and wealth. I want there to be a hospital, to go to sleep without worrying whether we will wake up tomorrow. I want children to be able to play in the streets, not run away from every sound.”

title: Nobody wants to rehome 'sweet' dog who loves cuddles because she's missing a leg
content:
Nobody wants to rehome 'sweet' dog who loves cuddles because she's missing a leg
An adorable six-year-old dog is searching for a new home in the UK after a devastating injury cost her one of her legs, could you offer her a place in your home and heart?
She has nothing but love to give, but sadly nobody wants to rehome one dog because of her disability. Little Mabel, a crossbreed, is searching for a new home in the UK after suffering unthinkable hardships on the streets of Egypt.
The six-year-old pooch was hit by a car and so badly injured that her front leg had to be amputated, leaving her to learn how to walk on three legs. Her rescuer said: "She really is the sweetest dog you could ever meet. She absolutely loves people and makes friends instantly, greeting everyone with a gentle heart and a wagging tail. She’s wonderful with other dogs too - playful, kind and easygoing.
"She adores cuddles and is incredibly affectionate. She just wants to be close, to feel safe and to be loved. With her gentle nature and loving personality, she truly is the perfect dog for any family."
She is up for adoption with Miracle’s Mission and can be rehomed anywhere in the UK, provided her new owner can meet her emotional and physical needs.
A statement reads: "Type of home needed - A loving home happy to adopt a dog with three legs.
"Mabel is spayed and weighs approximately 23kg. She is very sweet and loving.
"She was tested with cats and was nervous but not aggressive towards them at all."
Commenting on her rehoming post on Facebook, one user said: "Oh, you are a lovely, beautiful puppy. Come over here, you beautiful puppy."
Another user added: "Just a beautiful dog. Shared with optimism. I so wish I could take her.
"Had I not got two big dogs already, I would. The fact she’s a tripaw is not a problem, but I can't manage walking three dogs together and don’t really have time to do separate walks."
A third animal lover wrote: "I have a rescue from Egypt - she is a total sweetheart, a bit crazy, but she is a Golden Retriever. Shared for a great home."
While one more added: "My three-legged Melek from Macedonia is the biggest joy in my life. They do adapt and get on and just want to be happy. I also bet Mabel is a fast runner. These dogs are simply incredible. Good luck, Mabel."
Founded in 2015, Miracle’s Mission has been striving to "provide a place of safety for animals in danger, to educate on the need for neutering both pets and strays, and to neuter stray dogs and cats to prevent the birth of more animals onto the streets".
Explaining the origin of the charity’s name, a statement read: "Miracle was rescued from the streets of Borneo at one week old before her tiny eyes were even open. She was found with her siblings in a very remote area, where they were likely abandoned and very unwell.
"The puppies were full of maggots, with ringworm and blood parasites. Unfortunately, Miracle’s siblings did not make it.
"Miracle was cared for and had a very long road to recovery ahead of her. She had several injuries and was very weak, but she made a miraculous recovery and is now strong, happy and healthy, living with her adopted sisters Star and Tess, who were also rescued from Borneo. Her aim is to help save many more animals in need around the world.
"Miracle is the namesake of our charity, and her story is similar to many others around the world. However, not all of these animals are as lucky as she was in finding a loving home where she is spoilt rotten."
Anyone interested in rehoming Mabel, or another dog in Miracle’s Mission’s care, can email the team at info@miraclesmission.org.

title: Aigio shooting horror as 4 children blasted with shotgun and gunman on the loose
content:
A gunman is on the loose after firing a shotgun at four children.
The incident happened at around 2am on Filopoimenos Street in Aigio, Greece, when an unidentified man fired at the group as they were walking along the road.
A 15-year-old and three 16-year-olds suffered injuries to their legs and back during the attack. Emergency services rushed to the scene and the victims were taken to hospital for treatment.
The three 16-year-olds were admitted to Aigio Hospital, where doctors confirmed no life changing injuries. The 15-year-old was transferred to Karamandaneio Children’s Hospital in Patras and is also said to be in a stable condition.
Police have launched an investigation to identify and locate the suspect.

title: Putin official declared dead found after 13 years having 'faked her own death'
content:
Putin official declared dead found after 13 years having 'faked her own death'
A Russian official found guilty of corruption vanished before her sentencing 13 years ago - she was eventually found in the same area she committed her crimes
A former Vladimir Putin crony official who was declared dead 13 years ago has been found alive after allegedly going into hiding.
Marianna Stupina, a former first deputy minister of housing and communal services, vanished before she was due to be sentenced for corruption, in 2012. The ex-official, who worked in the Astrakhan region of southern Russia, allegedly went on the run over a decade ago to avoid serving seven years in jail. While in hiding, Stupina searched missing persons reports online and discovered the body of a woman resembling her has been discovered in Novosibirsk, Siberia, before she allegedly put together a plan.
Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.
Stupina had been convicted of several crimes including money laundering, large-scale embezzlement and the illegal production of payment documents, according to Russian site Baza.
When the ex-official came across the dead woman report she sent her husband to "identify" the body and say it was that of his wife, which closed the hunt.
She had fled to Tatarstan while in hiding, before boldly moving back to the region in southern Russia where she was an official after she was "declared dead".
Stupina incredibly spent 13 years in the same region she was wanted, before her luck and deception ran out. Investigators discovered the ex-official was alive then tracked her down and arrested her, this year.
The corrupt former deputy minister has since been pushed out a penal colony where she will serve out her sentence, more than a decade after her initial sentence. This comes after two Russian senior law enforcement officers were killed after a helicopter they were in mysteriously crashed.
The men, who were stated investigators, were found dead along with a "unlicensed" pilot in an "unregistered" chopper. A criminal investigation has been launched following the deadly crash, which happened around one mile from the takeoff location.
Last week, the defence chief of a NATO member state warned a Russian invasion cannot be ruled out. Norway's General Eirik Kristoffersen said much of evil Putin's nuclear arsenal is stationed on the Kola Peninsula in the Arctic, which is just a short distance from the Norwegian border.
Speaking to The Guardian: "We don't exclude a land grab from Russia as part of their plan to protect their own nuclear capabilities, which is the only thing they have left that actually threatens the United States."
Despite this, he added his country is not a target of the despot's territorial ambitions in the same way as Ukraine or other former Soviet states.

title: Cancer-stricken Brit, 66, fighting for life in India after horror on beach
content:
EXCLUSIVE: Cancer-stricken Brit, 66, fighting for life in India after horror on beach
The family of a beloved British man, who has fought lung cancer for six years, was left in shock after he suffered a terrifying stroke while on holiday with his wife
A retired fisherman is fighting for his life after a holiday with his beloved wife turned into a horror abroad.
Wayne Muress, 66, and his wife Maggie travelled to Goa, India, but disaster unexpectedly struck when the man, from Grimsby, became ill on an idyllic beach. The couple flew out to the popular holiday spot for a 21-day holiday to "enjoy life the best they can" after Wayne's lung cancer diagnosis six years ago - which forced him to retire from work. Wayne and Maggie were due to fly home on Saturday but the wife noticed something wrong with her husband's leg before he was rushed to hospital in India - where he remains.
Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.
Wayne's nephew's wife, Sam Piggott, told The Mirror that the beloved 66-year-old was put into an induced coma and underwent a crucial surgery, while his medical bills go into the thousands. The ex-fisherman had been feeling under the weather last week and was given antibiotics by a chemist in Goa for a possible chest infection and he started feeling better a day later, Sam said.
On Thursday, Wayne enjoyed breakfast in a shack then headed to the beach with his wife. The retired fisherman had been laying down but Maggie noticed something was wrong when her husband put his legs up but his right leg started shaking.
Sam said: "She'd said to him, 'What's up?' And he said, 'I don't know'. With that, his face dropped and his arm and the new one was having a stroke." She added: "The main sign was when his face started dropping... she knew something was out of character for him. She knows when he's poorly, obviously, because he suffers with a lung cancer, so she knows if he gets out of breath and stuff. But obviously, with his legs shaking and then his face drooping, they knew exactly what was happening."
Wayne was put onto a sun lounger by people on the beach and was rushed to hospital. The family revealed they do not yet know what brought on the horror stroke, just days before the couple were due to fly home.
The Brit was awake when initially rushed into hospital, but his condition deteriorated and was put into an induced coma. Sam told The Mirror: "On Thursday when he got rushed in, obviously he was really poorly. On Friday there wasn't really any change. On Saturday he lifted his leg... then on Sunday the doctors had gone in, spoken to him, he could tell him his name."
Wayne was put into an induced coma on Monday after he started suffering more seizures, Sam said. His family revealed the Brit underwent a tracheostomy - where an opening is made in the neck and a tube put into the windpipe to help breathing - and was taken out of the induced coma, but still has not woken up.
The family are now supporting Wayne's recovery and battling to bring him home from India. Maggie's first medical bill was a whopping £6,000 and his initial stay at a private hospital had been an extra £1,400 per day. Wayne has now been moved from the private clinic to a government hospital but his family revealed they think he is now also suffering from pneumonia.
A Gofundme page has been set up to help raise money for Wayne's treatment abroad and a repatriation flight in the future, which will be the next huge expense the family will deal with after the 66-year-old wakes up. The family said the money raised so far has gone to Wayne's medical costs and need funds to bring him home.
Sam revealed Maggie had travel insurance but Wayne had not taken out a claim due to his lung cancer. Sam said: "Wayne's currently going through cancer treatment as it is, he's had lung cancer for six years. So they didn't get travel insurance for Wayne because it was going to be in the thousands. So they just risked it. They've been to Goa quite a few times. So didn't think anything of it."
She added: "I'm just gutted, like, he was due to fly and I said if it was going to happen, why didn't it happen when he was in his own country?" Sam said Maggie and one of Wayne's best friends are in Goa with him during his recovery.
Sam revealed Maggie was "overwhelmed" by the Gofundme page and people's donations, and added: "They've been together 30 years. They spend all the time together, they do everything together, she's absolutely heartbroken... because obviously he's got the lung cancer, he keeps having good news about that and then this has happened."
On their holiday turned into a nightmare, she added: "With everything going on away, and obviously they're both getting older, they just like to enjoy this, they're just enjoying the life as best they can."
Sam added that Wayne was a massive West Ham fan, and he was pictured wearing The Hammers' clothes on his holiday. She said: "He's an avid West Ham supporter and be missing watching his matches." She added: "He's always worked all his life on 17 fishermen and even when the fishing stops in Grimsby he travelled far afield to provide for his family and his wife and he'd still be there if he wanted the cancer. An, the love of his community speaks volumes of the man he is."
To donate to the GoFundMe appeal, visit this link.

title: 'I'm horrified at celebs flaunting real fur - here's why their argument is crazy'
content:
'I'm horrified at celebs flaunting real fur - here's why their argument is crazy'
Anti-fur charity ACTAsia has spent two decades fighting to change the fashion industry - but now Gen Z's booming #FurReal TikTok trend could 'drag animal rights back decades', warns charity founder PEI SU
Pei Su, founder of anti-fur charity ACTAsia, says : "As big fashion labels take to the catwalk for London Fashion Week this weekend, I am thankful that once again, there isn't a single real fur trim in sight.
Anyone who has seen the horrors of fur farming first-hand as I have, would never dream of wanting fur to have a resurgence, yet that is what a growing number of celebrities and influencers are encouraging.
When London Fashion Week took the powerful step of going officially fur free in 2023, I celebrated, but never dreamt that just a few years later, some of the younger generation, who I often think of as the ones leading positive change, would be trying to give real fur a fresh lease of life.
Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.
READ MORE: 'Fur trade isn't just a danger to animals – it could cause next horror pandemic'READ MORE: UK suffers first-ever case of 'reverse zoonosis' as experts issue urgent pandemic warningThrough my work with my charity ACTAsia, I am well aware of the horror that is the fur industry, with tens of millions of foxes, mink, raccoon dogs bred and slaughtered each year. So imagine my horror to see so-called TikTok fashionistas, some of them with millions of followers, now giving airtime to fur. Then celebrities such as Kendall Jenner, Ice Spice and Rihanna have all stepped out in real fur in the past two years. Not exactly compassionate fashion.
Celebrities and influencers need to understand that fur farming is wrong on many levels. There is of course the appalling cruelty inflicted on animals, kept in tiny wire cages, killed via methods such as electrocution and gassing.
There is also the environmental impact of the intensely toxic and energy-consuming fur industry. As a comparison, the carbon footprint of producing 1kg of mink fur is 31 times that of cotton - how is that a good choice for our planet?
Then there are the global health implications. With so many animals crammed in together, who wouldn’t cross paths in the wild, and the human contact, fur farms are seen as high risk breeding grounds for pandemics.
One argument on social media is that vintage fur is good because it is 'recycling'. I don’t agree. All it does is normalise fur, making it socially acceptable in a way it hasn’t been for decades, therefore fuelling demand.
While the UK can pride itself on having banned fur farms long ago (England and Wales in 2000, Scotland and N Ireland in 2002) it is a frustrating loophole that imports and sales are still allowed. Stores and designers such as Harrods and Louis Vuitton still sell furs in the UK. How can we really allow this if we believe the industry is wrong? Is it acceptable so long as the cruelty happens in another country, such as China, (the world’s largest producer of fur), Finland, or the US?
If Brits want to show they condemn fur, I would encourage them to throw their support behind a bill in the Houses of Parliament calling for the ban of import and sale of fur. Brought by MP Ruth Jones, it is due for a second reading on February 27.
A lot of ACTAsia's work in changing the culture around fur focuses on China because of its role as a key supplier on the global stage. We want consumers and professionals to make informed choices by shining a light on the industry. Thankfully our biannual reports show that the number of fur farms in China are falling, but in 2024 there were still 10.76 million pelts produced.
Other positive steps have seen over 1600 of retailers around the world sign up to the Fur Free Retailers scheme, of which ACTAsia has recruited 112 in China.
LFW took a further step and banned exotic animal skins from 2024, and New York Fashion Week is fur free from September. Milan and Paris Fashion Weeks still allow big name brands such as Fendi to showcase fur, but have lost sponsorship and face growing opposition.
So while the UK is doing well on the catwalks this week, out on the street I am concerned we are seeing a different story. I would urge everyone to think carefully about their choices, before a return to fur drags the UK back several decades when it comes to animal rights."
Pei is the founder and CEO of ACTAsia, a UN-backed NGO, focusing on increasing people's compassion for animals, people and the planet. It is a leading expert on Chinese fur farms, thanks to its grassroots, on-the-ground investigations and biannual reports. For more on ACTAsia's work, see www.actasia.org

title: German man 'deliberately pushed Brit to death from Lanzarote shopping centre'
content:
A German man is accused of deliberately pushing a Brit off the top of a shopping centre, killing him.
The 59-year-old faces a lengthy prison term if found guilty, but he denies responsibility, saying he was acting in self defence and that the shopping centre is to blame for its lack of security measures. The British man, identified only by his initials of TCJ, suffered serious injuries and later died in hospital in Lanzarote, Spain.
The incident happened in the popular holiday resort of Costa Teguise in May 2023.
A trial is now underway and will continue into next week. The defence has asked for the defendant to be acquitted or to be sentenced to just two years in prison for reckless homicide. If eventual intent is found, they argue the man should spend two years and six months in prison, considering that mitigating circumstances concur.
The defence team claim the German man acted in self-defence when he was "the object of a brutal attack". But both the prosecution and a private case brought by the Brit's family want a lengthy prison sentence as they claim the German man's intention was to end the life of TCJ.
The Prosecutor's Office asks that a sentence of 12 years in prison be imposed and the victim's family claims the maximum for homicide, 15 years. Both parties request the payment of €120,000 in civil liability, according to Spanish newspaper La Voz de Lanzarote.
The defence argues the German man is a "normal person, without a criminal record" and plans to show a video of the incident in court. They say his intention was to only invite the deceased to have a drink, but he, along with another British man, assaulted him and he acted in self-defence.
The prosecutor says the death of TCJ "was not the result of bad luck or chance" . The British victim "was not a drunkard, nor did he want to attack the accused".
The trial, held in Gran Canaria, continues.

title: Fire rips through World Legacy Cruise ship as one killed and hundreds evacuated
content:
A fire on a cruise ship left one person dead and four others in hospital after nearly 600 people were forced to evacuate.
According to the Maritime and Port Authority of Singapore, the blaze broke out around 4.30am on February 20 in the lounge area of the World Legacy Cruise ship as it was sailing towards Singapore.
A 23-year-old Indonesian crew member, identified only as PL, died in the incident. The deceased man has been transferred to land and the Indonesian Embassy has been informed by the authorities. The cause of the fire is unknown and is currently under investigation.
Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.
Fire crews found a laundry attendant collapsed in the lobby on deck nine, authorities confirmed. At the time the blaze broke out, there were 271 passengers and 388 crew members on board the ship.
Witnesses have told of their horror, with one passenger describing the evacuation as a “a truly terrible and traumatising experience.”
She told The Straits Times: “Everyone panicked, that’s when the loudspeaker announced the fire and asked everyone to move to deck seven.” The tourist said smoke was already moving onto deck three at the time.
“When we reached deck seven, there were crew [members] standing at the door, asking everyone to put on life vests and move to the outside deck,” she said.
MPA said the fire was extinguished by the Singapore Civil Defense Force, but this was six hours after it initially began, initially being contained by the crew on board. A safety boundary was set around the ship and the MPA sent out navigational alerts to other vessels in the area to stay clear.
Surveyors instructed by World Legacy will board the ship to assess the extent of the damage. In the meantime, there is a core crew on board to assist with the current operations and assist with matters of the ongoing investigation into how exactly the fire came to be.

title: Tourist killed on bike in Mallorca as he's dragged across road and run over twice
content:
Tourist killed on bike in Mallorca as he's dragged across road and run over twice
The tourist was struck by a van, dragged along the road and hit again moments later before emergency services arrived at the scene and sadly pronounced the man dead
A tourist in his 40s has been killed while cycling after being struck by two vehicles.
Police in Mallorca said the cyclist rode through a stop sign at a junction, where he was hit by traffic travelling in the opposite direction. A Volkswagen Transporter van struck him first, sending him into the air and dragging him along the road. Shortly after, he was hit again by a Peugeot Rifter.
Emergency crews raced to the area, but despite their best efforts, the tourist could not be saved and was tragically pronounced dead. The collision, which happened on the Ma-3030 motorway, between Santa Maria del Camí and Santa Eugènia, left one driver with wounds to the face.
The man had been staying in the Spanish resort of Playa de Palma while on a cycling trip. His nationality has not yet been confirmed, and local police have opened a full investigation into the fatal crash.
Debris from the collision was clearly visible at the scene, including the man's heavily damaged bike and a shattered windscreen on the Volkswagen van involved.
In April last year, a 59-year-old Irish cyclist died in Mallorca after collapsing and falling off his bike on the Ma-2210 road between Port de Pollensa and the Formentor Lighthouse.
In the same month, a Brit cyclist, 40, also died whilst taking part in the iconic Mallorca 312 race, where he had been riding alongside Chelsea legend Eden Hazard. The man came off his bike during the amateur road event, which circles the island and finishes in Playa de Muro.
A statement from event organisers read: "Mallorca 312 OK Mobility deeply regrets the death of a participant during the celebration of the event after suffering a fall.
"We want to offer our most sincere condolences to the rider’s family and loved ones. We have already made ourselves available to offer them all the support they need at these difficult moments. Rest in peace."

title: Missing Brit found dead in Benidorm hospital after freak accident
content:
Missing Brit found dead in Benidorm hospital after freak accident
A 54-year-old British man was tragically found dead in a Spanish hospital after a horror accident - missing posters claimed he was seen leaving the facility
A British man has been found dead in a Benidorm hospital after a horror accident.
Pete Jackson, 54, is said to have suffocated after getting trapped in a gap filled with electrical cables between a patio wall and a metal platform. Police believe the expat could have got into difficulties after trying to recover personal belongings and ended up "upside down" with no way out.
News of his death emerged last week when his wife Shirley Ebbs shared news of the tragedy, but harrowing details of his death emerged today.
Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.
READ MORE: 'Wonderful' nurse killed on way to work in Limerick three car smash picturedREAD MORE: Boy, 2, dies after being accidentally run over while playing on family’s drivewayReports around the time he went missing said Pete had lived and worked in Benidorm for nearly three years - and discharged himself from the Benidorm hospital where he was found dead on February 7 while he was still on medication.
Today respected Costa Blanca newspaper Informacion said Mr Jackson had been found inside the hospital installations, despite reported sightings of him leaving the hospital.
A missing poster published online before his body was found said: "Pete was last seen leaving IMED Levante Hospital in Benidorm heading for home. We are yet to ascertain from police which direction he went on leaving the hospital.
"He has been quite frail and disorientated. This is completely out of character for Peter and he has never been without contact with his family."
Although initially police believe he could have been electrocuted because he was found trapped near cables, an autopsy showed he had suffocated.
Local press reported his wife had turned up at the hospital where he was a patient after finishing work and raised the alarm after discovering he wasn't in his room. An initial search of the hospital showed up no signs of where he might be. His body was found on the sixth-floor of the hospital.
Wife Shirley, in a Facebook post, said: "It is with a heavy heart that I share an update on Pete Jackson. Pete has been located, but unfortunately, he has been found deceased."
A friend added: "Shirley Ebbs wants to thank everyone who helped in the search and offered support during this incredibly difficult time. Please keep Pete Jackson's family in your thoughts and respect their privacy right now."

title: 'Devil' dad who hacked son, 10, to death in Tenerife machete bloodbath pictured
content:
A man who killed his son before attacking police in a holiday hotspot has been pictured for the first time.
A 10-year-old boy was murdered by his father, named by local media as Juan Manuel Alonso Fumero, in a machete attack in the village of Cabo Blanco, Tenerife. The boy has been named locally as Yared.
Fumero, 35, was later shot dead by the Civil Guard after chasing residents and officers while armed. The boy’s mother, Almudena, 26, fought against her ex-husband to save her child and is currently fighting for her life at the Nuestra Señora de La Candelaria University Hospital after suffering severe injuries.
Three neighbours rushed to help Almudena and her son after hearing screams but they were relentlessly pursued. Witnesses described scenes of terror, saying: "It was the devil in action in a horror movie."
Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.
Fernando, a neighbour in Cabo Blanco, said he heard screaming and found the home covered in blood but blocked by an external grille. Another neighbour described the scene as chaotic: "What we saw was horrible, there are no words, I will not forget it in my life."
The group forced entry and confronted the armed attacker, who had a 60cm machete. The neighbours were followed by the police who shot Fumero several times according to witnesses.
Fernando said: "With the help of a friend and my brother, we managed to break down the door… I think that saved the woman's life."
He added that the father turned on them, forcing them to flee: "He caught me… and hit me with a machete on the finger… the policemen were coming… they turned and shot him several times. If it were not for that, the disaster would have been much greater."
Inside the property, officials said the child was found with a "very serious wound to the head", while Almudena was in a "tremendous, frightening state" with severe injuries to her limbs. She lost significant blood and was rushed to hospital for emergency surgery, with doctors hoping she will survive.
