Authorities said the father had no known criminal record or complaints. Police added he tried to attack officers in a state of extreme agitation, injuring one in the arm. The wound was caused when his blade struck the edge of the officer’s bulletproof vest, requiring minor surgery.
The Civil Guard is investigating whether drugs played a role. Sources described the scene as one of "extreme harshness" and said the attacks were "especially shocking".
Neighbours told Diario de Avisos they have struggled to sleep since the tragedy. Many described the attacker as behaving "as if possessed" and said they believe more people would have been killed if police had not opened fire.
The main Canarian institutions observed a minute of silence to condemn the murder and the council of Arona, where the family is from, has decreed two days of official mourning in the municipality as a sign of pain, respect and solidarity. A minute of silence was also observed in the square of Arona.
President Fernando Clavijo described the incident as "absolutely horrifying and dramatic" while mayor Fátima Lemes stressed the municipality’s "firm commitment" to protecting children.

title: Senior Russian cops die in helicopter crash horror after probing mystery death
content:
Three people including two senior law enforcement officers have been killed after their helicopter crashed.
The Robinson R44 went missing soon after leaving a logging site around 80 miles from the nearest village of Amarka in Russia. On board were a police chief and a Russian Investigative Committee detective who had examined the body of a deceased worker and collected evidence relating to his death.
The state investigators died together with a pilot who was described as “unlicensed”, while flying a helicopter that was “unregistered.” A criminal investigation has been launched into the unusual crash. The bodies of the three people on board were located among the burned remains of the Robinson R44 helicopter.
Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.
“During search and rescue operations, a destroyed helicopter and the bodies of the deceased pilot and two passengers were discovered approximately [one mile]from the takeoff site,” said the Blagoveshchensk Transport Prosecutor's Office.
“It has now been established that the aircraft belonged to the deceased pilot, who was not licensed to fly it.
“The flight was conducted without notifying air navigation authorities. The helicopter was not officially registered in accordance with the established procedure.”
This comes after Russia issued a chilling warning to the West, as a leading politician declared: "Two can play at that game."
Nationalist politician and Vladimir Putin ally Alexey Zhuravlev suggested Russia should register its cargo ships as military assets, meaning action against them from Western forces would constitute "full-scale military action" in his eyes.
American, French and even British forces have been involved in actions against Vladimir Putin's so-called 'shadow fleet' of oil tankers attempting to circumvent sanctions on Russia.
Zhuravlev outlined a series of aggressive measures Moscow could take to protect its shipping in a post on Telegram. Speaking about Russia's maritime cargo, which he said has been "subject to increasing attacks recently", Zhuravlev said: "We could, of course, assign armed guards to every tanker, but in that case, we would need to be prepared to use these weapons."
He added: "I advocate doing this without much deliberation, and equipping each vessel with at least one long-range machine gun—this would immediately make our adversaries more cautious."
Zhuravlev went on to suggest Russia could formally militarise its commercial fleet, warning this would significantly escalate any confrontation at sea. "We could, in principle, legally register the entire fleet as part of the military—and then an attack on it would naturally be fraught with full-scale military action, even if they tried," he said.
He ended his message with a direct threat aimed at Western nations, declaring: "And then two can play this game. Impose sanctions on Western cargo ships, stop them, and seize their cargo for the benefit of the Russian state. For the Motherland!"

Global business, politics

While the Financial Times is renowned for its award-winning business reporting, the topics we cover are much broader than many realise.

OPINION
Expert reporting
and opinion.
Our network of 700+ journalists report from all over the world, ensuring a truly global view of key events and decoding their impact on global business, finance, economics and politics.
Roula Khalaf
Roula Khalaf is Editor of the Financial Times. She was previously deputy editor from 2016 to 2020, overseeing a range of newsroom initiatives and award winning editorial projects and leading a global network of over 100 foreign correspondents.
John Burn-Murdoch
John Burn-Murdoch is the Financial Times’ Chief Data Reporter. He led the FT’s data-driven coverage of the pandemic, exploring its impacts on health, the economy and wider society. When pandemics are not happening, he uses data and graphics to tell stories on topics including politics, economics, climate change and sport, and is a visiting lecturer at the London School of Economics.
Stephen Bush
Stephen Bush is an associate editor and columnist at the Financial Times. He writes a daily newsletter, Inside Politics, charting the course of politics and policy in the United Kingdom, and a wide-ranging weekly column.
Elaine Moore
Elaine Moore is Tech comment editor at the Financial Times. Previously, she was deputy editor of the FT’s Lex column and wrote commentary on the technology industry from the San Francisco bureau. Prior to this she covered global government debt from London, with a particular focus on the debt crisis in Greece, Ukraine and Venezuela.
Pilita Clark
Pilita Clark is an associate editor and business columnist at the FT where she writes on corporate life and climate change. Formerly the FT’s environment correspondent, her writing has won awards in the US and Asia and in 2019 she was named Environment Journalist of the Year for the third year in a row at the British Press Awards.
Sarah O'Connor
Sarah O'Connor is a columnist, reporter and associate editor at the Financial Times. She writes a weekly column focused on the world of work, as well as longer reported articles. She joined the FT in 2007 and has covered the US economy from Washington DC, the UK economy from London and the financial crisis from Iceland.
Edward Luce
Edward Luce is the US national editor and columnist at the Financial Times. Before that he was the FT's Washington Bureau chief. Other roles have included South Asia bureau chief, Capital Markets editor, and Philippines Correspondent.
Enuma Okoro
Enuma Okoro is a monthly columnist for FT Weekend's Life & Arts. She writes on the intersection of arts, culture and life. Based in New York, she is a writer, speaker and cultural curator.
Anjana Ahuja
Anjana Ahuja is a contributing writer on science, offering weekly opinion on significant developments in global science, health and technology. She was previously a feature writer and columnist at The Times in London.
Lucy Fisher
Lucy Fisher is Whitehall editor at the Financial Times and host of the FT's Political Fix podcast. She focuses on UK politics, the civil service, and foreign and defence policy.
She is the author of two acclaimed books, Emily Wilding Davison: The Martyr Suffragette (2018) and Women in the War: The Last Heroines of Britain’s Greatest Generation (2021). Fisher is also an associate fellow at the Royal United Services Institute (RUSI), the world's oldest defence and security think tank.
Rana Foroohar
Rana Foroohar is Global Business Columnist and an Associate Editor at the Financial Times, based in New York. She is also CNN’s global economic analyst.
Janan Ganesh
Janan Ganesh is a biweekly columnist and associate editor for the FT. He writes on international politics for the FT and culture for FT Weekend. He was previously political correspondent for The Economist for five years.
Robert Shrimsley
Robert Shrimsley is UK chief political commentator and UK editor at large of the Financial Times. He writes a weekly column on British politics and for the FT weekend magazine. Before this, he served as the FT’s chief political correspondent, news editor, managing editor of FT.com and editorial director.
Martin Wolf
Martin Wolf is chief economics commentator at the Financial Times, London. He was awarded the CBE (Commander of the British Empire) in 2000 “for services to financial journalism”.
